~]S<: Guide for using American Fuzzy Lop to find multiple vulnerabilities into an open-source project
title: Fuzzing an Open-Source Project with AFL
The first step that needs to be completed is the setup of the fuzzing environment. As the manual installation could be a draining process, it can be used an unofficial Docker image (namely
mykter/afl-training
c
a
https://hub.docker.com/r/mykter/afl-training/dockerfile
) with all the tools already installed. This was primarily used for a workshop in which concepts about the fuzzer American Fuzzy Lop (AFL) are introduced via practical exercises.
Install Docker by running the official installation script.
Pull the image and create a container (in the privileged mode, with the
c
port exposed, and a password set as environment variable).
Log in into the created container, by using the
fuzzer
c
account with the set password and the
c
port.
Finding and Compiling (with Instrumentation) an Open-Source Project
In this guide, due to the different build processes implemented by each project, a specific open-source one was chosen:
HiColor
a
https://github.com/dbohdan/hicolor
. It is a quite popular (judging by the stars on GitHub) program for converting PNG images into 15- or 16-bit RBG color ones.
Clone the project into the home of the
fuzzer
c
user (the default directory after the above login).

AFL could benefit from the fact that the source code is available, for performing a fuzzing in the whitebox approach. For achieving this, the binary needs to incorporate instrumentation artifacts, so the compilation needs to be a little altered. Optionally, the Makefile could be edited by adding the
g
c
option to the
CFLAG
c
variables to include debug symbols into the executable.
As the fuzzer would be helped by valid inputs from which it could later mutate for generating new inputs, sample PNG images could be offered to AFL.
Create a directory for storing sample images.
Manually download 5 PNG images in the newly created directory.
Solve the AFL errors (only if it is required).

export AFL_I_DONT_CARE_ABOUT_MISSING_CRASHES=1
echo performance \
  | sudo tee /sys/devices/system/cpu/cpu*/cpufreq/scaling_governor
Start the fuzzing for the encoding functionality
Start the fuzzing for the decoding functionality
Start the fuzzing for the custom format parsing functionality
After running the last 3 commands, the following outputs are generated:

From this information, a coarse conclusion can be formed: only the encoding functionality has a poor implementation, due to the multiple unique crashes that AFL could find. For the rest of the functionalities, namely the decoding and the custom format parsing, the fuzzer does not find any vulnerability.
The first generated crash was chosen and further analyzed with dynamic techniques, namely debugging.
gdb
c

was instructed to automatically start the program for encoding, with the file that generated the first crash. It needs to be mentioned that the names of the dumped files could vary depending on multiple factors (order of finding crashes, time, current operation).
The line on which the program generates a
SIGSERV
c
is the one highlighted below. It tries (and fails) to dereference the
rgb_img
c
pointer, which is
NULL
c

|   29   hicolor_rgb* cp_to_rgb(const cp_image_t img)                                │
│   30   {                                                                           │
│   31       hicolor_rgb* rgb_img = malloc(sizeof(hicolor_rgb) * img.w * img.h);     │
│   32                                                                               │
│   33       for (uint32_t i = 0; i < (uint32_t) img.w * (uint32_t) img.h; i++) {    │
│  >34           rgb_img[i].r = img.pix[i].r;                                        │
│   35           rgb_img[i].g = img.pix[i].g;                                        │
│   36           rgb_img[i].b = img.pix[i].b;                                        │
│   37       }                                                                       │
│   38                                                                               │
│   39       return rgb_img;                                                         │
│   40   }
By inspecting the parameters used in the
malloc
c
call (with the command
p img
c
), huge values can be observed. This means that the product on the line 31 results in 34582635452664. This value is in fact not truncated (it can be stored into an
unsigned int
c
, on a 64-bit architecture) and the
malloc
c
is instructed to allocate 31 terabytes on the stack, it fails and returns a
NULL
c
pointer.
The PNG image that generated the crash can be analyzed with
file
c
, which only parses the header of the file, or with
identify
c
, a utility from the ImageMagick suite, which investigate the PNG file more deeply.

file encode_results/default/crashes/id:000000,sig:11,src:001013,time:93879,op:havoc,rep:2
encode_results/default/crashes/id:000000,sig:11,src:001013,time:93879,op:havoc,rep:2: PNG image data, 62463 x 184549976, 8-bit/color RGB, non-interlaced

identify ../crashes/id:000007,sig:11,src:001013,time:93879,op:havoc,rep:2
identify-im6.q16: insufficient image data in file `../crashes/id:000007,sig:11,src:001013,time:93879,op:havoc,rep:2' @ error/png.c/ReadPNGImage/4098.
By correlating this information with the
source code
a
https://github.com/dbohdan/hicolor/blob/master/cli.c
of the analyzed program, the root cause can be reached. AFL, in its input generation phase, modified the sizes (height and width) stored in the PNG header with the large sizes seen in the
file
c
command output. The project uses an open-source header-only C library (namely
cute_png.h
c
) to loads the PNG files.
Further, both the library and
hicolor
c
does not check if the image is valid (like
identify
c
, which detects that the declared pixels from the header are not stored in the data section too) and the image is loaded as it is. When the image needs to be converted to the custom file format, the
malloc
c
fails and the programs tries to dereference a
NULL
c
pointer, which raises a
SIGSERV
c
The bug discovered in this guide was reported by creating a
new issue
a
https://github.com/dbohdan/hicolor/issues/2
on the repository.
